protocol_definition.name: Unstructured Group Chat
protocol_definition.message_flow: Agents freely contribute to a shared conversation; Writer finalizes.
protocol_definition.visibility: Every agent sees the task and complete shared conversation history.
protocol_definition.termination: Stop after the configured discussion rounds and Writer output.

final_call.expected_action: Produce a self-contained final answer in the required format. Return the deliverable itself, not a transcript excerpt, role report, critique, or plan.

intermediate_messages.content:
1. Executive Summary and Assumptions
We will launch a pilot AI course-QA assistant for two undergraduate courses (~300 students) over 12 weeks. The assistant answers only from instructor-approved materials, with safeguards against hallucination, privacy breaches, academic integrity violations, and escalation to human staff. The team: one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one TA, and a $5,000 budget. A contingency at Week 5 forces one developer to be unavailable Weeks 6-8 and reallocates $750 from infrastructure to privacy review/testing. Total budget remains ≤$5,000.

**Assumptions:**
- Instructor-approved course materials are provided by Week 2.
- University IT provides basic cloud hosting (e.g., AWS Educate) at no extra cost; infrastructure line item covers only additional compute/storage.
- Privacy review is conducted by the TA with faculty oversight, not external legal.
- Student developers work 10-15 hours/week each; TA works 5 hours/week; faculty sponsor 2 hours/week.
- No existing AI assistant platform is reused; we build from open-source components.
- No university policies or approvals are invented; we assume standard academic integrity and data privacy rules apply.

2. Goals and Non-Goals
**Goals:**
- Deploy a functional AI assistant answering from approved course materials by Week 8.
- Achieve ≤5% hallucination rate on a held-out test set by Week 10.
- Handle at least 100 student queries per week by Week 12.
- Maintain zero privacy breaches and zero academic integrity violations during pilot.
- Escalate all unanswerable or sensitive queries to human staff within 2 hours.

**Non-Goals:**
- Not building a general-purpose chatbot.
- Not integrating with university LMS beyond basic API (if available).
- Not supporting more than two courses or 300 students.
- Not achieving production-level reliability or 24/7 uptime.

3. Stakeholder and Decision-Rights Map
| Stakeholder | Role | Decision Rights |
|-------------|------|-----------------|
| Faculty Sponsor | Part-time, approves content | Final approval on course materials, escalation protocol, and go/no-go at milestones |
| Student Developer A | Lead developer | Technical architecture, code implementation, testing |
| Student Developer B | Support developer | Documentation, integration, backup for A |
| Teaching Assistant (TA) | Privacy & testing lead | Privacy review, test case design, escalation handling |
| Students (300) | End users | Provide feedback via surveys (no decision rights) |
| Planner (you) | Strategic oversight | Recommend scope changes, budget reallocation, and stop criteria |

4. Baseline 12-Week Roadmap Table
| Week | Deliverable | Owner | Dependency |
|------|-------------|-------|------------|
| 1 | Project kickoff, define scope, assign roles | Faculty Sponsor | None |
| 2 | Collect approved course materials (syllabi, readings, FAQs) | Faculty Sponsor | Week 1 |
| 3 | Build core retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline | Dev A | Week 2 |
| 4 | Integrate hallucination guardrails (confidence threshold, source citation) | Dev A | Week 3 |
| 5 | Privacy review of data handling (no storage of student PII) | TA | Week 3 |
| 6 | Unit testing of answer accuracy on 50 sample queries | Dev B | Week 4 |
| 7 | Integration testing with escalation workflow (human handoff) | Dev A, TA | Week 5, 6 |
| 8 | Soft launch to 50 beta students; collect initial feedback | All | Week 7 |
| 9 | Iterate based on beta feedback (fix top 3 issues) | Dev A, Dev B | Week 8 |
| 10 | Full launch to 300 students; monitor hallucination rate | All | Week 9 |
| 11 | Gather usage data, survey students, analyze escalations | TA | Week 10 |
| 12 | Final report, decision gate (Expand/Revise/Stop) | Faculty Sponsor | Week 11 |

5. Baseline Resource and Budget Allocation
| Resource | Allocation | Budget |
|----------|------------|--------|
| Faculty Sponsor | 2 hrs/week (unpaid) | $0 |
| Student Developer A | 15 hrs/week (stipend) | $2,000 |
| Student Developer B | 10 hrs/week (stipend) | $1,500 |
| TA | 5 hrs/week (stipend) | $500 |
| Infrastructure (cloud compute, storage) | - | $1,000 |
| **Total** | | **$5,000** |

6. Week 6-8 Contingency Overlay and Ownership Changes
**Contingency:** At end of Week 5, Student Developer B becomes unavailable Weeks 6-8. $750 from infrastructure is reallocated to privacy review and testing.

**Scope changes:**
- Weeks 6-8: Integration testing and soft launch are delayed by one week (shift to Weeks 7-9). Full launch moves to Week 11, final report to Week 13 (but we keep 12-week window; see below).
- Ownership: TA takes over documentation and basic testing from Dev B. Dev A handles all coding and integration alone. Faculty sponsor increases to 3 hrs/week to assist with escalation workflow design.
- Dependencies: Privacy review (Week 5) is now extended into Week 6 using reallocated funds for deeper testing. Soft launch (originally Week 8) moves to Week 9.
- Testing: Additional $750 funds TA to run 200 test queries (vs. 50) and conduct a formal privacy audit of data flows.
- Support: During Weeks 6-8, escalation is handled by TA and faculty sponsor only; Dev A focuses on code.
- Milestones: Beta feedback collection moves from Week 8 to Week 9; full launch from Week 10 to Week 11.
- Decision gates: Week 8 gate (originally after beta) moves to Week 9; Week 12 gate remains but now evaluates a shorter full-launch period (Weeks 11-12 only).

**Note:** To stay within 12 weeks, we compress Weeks 11-12: gather data and final report in same week. This reduces data collection time but is acceptable for a pilot.

7. Revised Budget Showing the Exact $750 Reallocation
| Item | Baseline | Revised | Change |
|------|----------|---------|--------|
| Dev A stipend | $2,000 | $2,000 | $0 |
| Dev B stipend | $1,500 | $1,500 | $0 |
| TA stipend | $500 | $500 | $0 |
| Infrastructure | $1,000 | $250 | -$750 |
| Privacy review & testing (new) | $0 | $750 | +$750 |
| **Total** | **$5,000** | **$5,000** | **$0** |

8. Risk Register and Mitigations
| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|------------|--------|------------|
| Hallucination rate >5% | Medium | High | Confidence threshold; human-in-loop for low-confidence answers |
| Privacy breach (e.g., student data leaked) | Low | Critical | No storage of PII; all logs anonymized; TA reviews weekly |
| Academic integrity violation (e.g., assistant gives answers) | Medium | High | Only answer from approved materials; flag "homework help" queries to human |
| Developer unavailability (contingency) | High | Medium | Cross-train TA; faculty sponsor steps in; scope compression |
| Budget overrun | Low | Medium | Fixed $5,000; reallocation only within approved categories |
| Low student adoption | Medium | Low | Beta group incentives (extra credit?); faculty announcement |

9. Success Metrics and Data-Collection Plan
**Metrics:**
- Hallucination rate: % of answers with incorrect or unsourced info (measured weekly via random sample of 50 queries).
- Query volume: total queries per week (logged automatically).
- Escalation rate: % of queries escalated to human (target <10%).
- Student satisfaction: survey at Week 12 (Likert scale 1-5).
- Privacy incidents: count of any PII exposure (target 0).
- Academic integrity incidents: count of reported misuse (target 0).

**Data collection:**
- Automated logging of all queries and responses (anonymized).
- Weekly manual review of 50 random query-answer pairs by TA.
- Survey at Week 12 (Google Form, anonymous).
- Faculty sponsor tracks escalation logs.

10. Governance, Content Approval, and Escalation Process
- **Content approval:** All course materials must be approved by faculty sponsor before ingestion. Updates require re-approval.
- **Escalation:** If assistant cannot answer (confidence <0.7) or query is flagged as sensitive (e.g., "exam answer"), it routes to TA within 1 hour. TA responds or escalates to faculty sponsor within 2 hours.
- **Privacy:** No student names, IDs, or emails stored. Logs are anonymized and deleted after 30 days.
- **Academic integrity:** Assistant will not answer direct homework or exam questions; it will only point to relevant approved readings. Faculty sponsor reviews all flagged queries weekly.

11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop Decision Criteria (After Week 12)
**Expand (proceed to full deployment):**
- Hallucination rate ≤3% for 2 consecutive weeks.
- Student satisfaction score ≥4.0/5.0.
- Zero privacy or integrity incidents.
- Query volume ≥150/week.
- Faculty sponsor approves.

**Revise/Extend (continue pilot with changes):**
- Hallucination rate 3-5% OR satisfaction 3.0-3.9 OR query volume 100-149/week.
- Requires scope revision (e.g., add more guardrails, improve training data) and 4-week extension.

**Stop:**
- Hallucination rate >5% after Week 10.
- Any privacy breach or academic integrity violation.
- Budget overrun >$5,000.
- Faculty sponsor decides to stop.

12. Final Recommendation
Proceed with the baseline plan, but immediately prepare for the contingency by cross-training the TA on testing and documentation in Weeks 1-5. The $750 reallocation from infrastructure to privacy/testing is a prudent trade-off: reduced compute capacity (use free-tier cloud) for stronger safeguards. The compressed Weeks 11-12 are acceptable for a pilot. The decision criteria after Week 12 provide clear, measurable exit ramps.

**Researcher Contribution:**

I want to flag a critical dependency gap in the baseline plan that the contingency overlay does not fully address: **the Week 3-4 pipeline build assumes both developers are available for pair debugging of the RAG retrieval layer.** With Dev B absent Weeks 6-8, any unresolved technical debt from Weeks 3-4 will compound during integration testing (now shifted to Week 7-8 under contingency).

**Evidence from the plan:**
- Baseline Week 3: "Build core RAG pipeline" owned by Dev A, but dependency on Week 2 materials.
- Baseline Week 4: "Integrate hallucination guardrails" owned by Dev A.
- Contingency: Dev A works alone Weeks 6-8; TA takes documentation/testing but has no coding background (assumed from TA stipend level).

**Recommendation to mitigate:**
- In Weeks 1-2, have Dev A document the RAG pipeline architecture in a shared wiki that TA can use to run manual test cases during Weeks 6-8.
- Add a **Week 4.5 checkpoint** (not in original roadmap) where Dev A and Dev B pair-review the guardrail logic before Dev B's potential absence. This is a low-cost dependency buffer.

**Assumption to label explicitly:**
- TA has no prior coding experience; their testing role is limited to black-box query validation, not code-level debugging. If this assumption is wrong, the contingency risk decreases.

**Non-duplicative addition:** The current risk register lists "Developer unavailability" but does not quantify the **technical debt accumulation risk** from solo development. I propose adding a metric: *"Number of unresolved pull requests or known bugs at Week 5"* as a leading indicator for whether the contingency is survivable.

The key insight is that the baseline plan’s Week 3-4 pipeline build creates latent technical debt that the contingency overlay (Weeks 6-8) does not address, and the Researcher’s recommendation for a Week 4.5 checkpoint is sound but needs explicit ownership and a measurable trigger.

**Proposed Adjustment to Contingency Plan:**

- **Add a Week 4.5 checkpoint (end of Week 4):** Dev A and Dev B conduct a 2-hour pair review of the hallucination guardrail logic. This is a dependency buffer, not a new deliverable. No budget impact.
- **Ownership change during Weeks 6-8:** The TA, under faculty sponsor oversight, will run a pre-defined set of 50 black-box test cases (provided by Dev A in Week 5) against the pipeline. This tests for regression without requiring coding skills. The $750 reallocation funds the TA’s extra time for this (10 hours at $75/hour equivalent, within the $750 privacy/testing line).
- **New leading indicator metric:** Track “number of unresolved pull requests or known bugs at Week 5” as a risk trigger. If >3 unresolved items, escalate to faculty sponsor for a scope reduction decision before Week 6.

**Revised Risk Register Entry (non-duplicative addition):**

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|------------|--------|------------|
| Technical debt from solo development (Weeks 6-8) | High | Medium | Week 4.5 pair review; TA runs pre-defined black-box tests; faculty sponsor monitors unresolved bug count at Week 5 |

**Budget Impact:** Zero. The $750 reallocation already covers TA overtime for testing; the Week 4.5 checkpoint uses existing developer hours.

**Conclusion:** The Researcher’s dependency gap is real. By adding a low-cost checkpoint and shifting TA testing scope, we keep the compressed timeline viable without exceeding budget or relying on unstated TA coding skills. The leading indicator metric gives an early stop-or-adjust trigger before the contingency period begins.

intermediate_messages.content:
**Critic Response:**

I identify three specific issues in the current plan that require correction or stronger justification.

**1. Budget reallocation inconsistency: The $750 from infrastructure is not actually available.**  
The baseline budget lists $1,000 for infrastructure. The contingency reallocates $750 from that line to privacy review/testing, leaving $250. However, the plan assumes "university IT provides basic cloud hosting (e.g., AWS Educate) at no extra cost." If that assumption holds, the infrastructure line item is *already* zero-cost for basic hosting. The $1,000 baseline allocation must therefore cover *additional* compute/storage beyond free tier. Cutting it to $250 without specifying what is sacrificed (e.g., reduced GPU time, smaller vector database) is a scope change that is not addressed. **Correction:** Explicitly state what infrastructure is lost (e.g., "Reduced to single-instance deployment; no load testing possible") and confirm the free tier covers the pilot's needs. Otherwise, the budget is misleading.

**2. The contingency timeline compression is mathematically flawed.**  
The plan shifts soft launch to Week 9 and full launch to Week 11, then claims Weeks 11-12 are compressed into one week for data gathering and final report. But Week 12 is the final week; if full launch is Week 11, you have only Week 11-12 (two weeks) of full-launch data, not one. The plan says "gather data and final report in same week" but does not specify which week. If Week 12 is both data collection and reporting, that is feasible only if data collection is automated and analysis is trivial. **Correction:** State explicitly: "Full launch occurs Week 11. Data collection runs Weeks 11-12. Final report is drafted in Week 12 using automated dashboards; manual analysis is deferred to Week 13 (unfunded)." This avoids implying a one-week miracle.

**3. The "Expand" criterion hallucination rate ≤3% is inconsistent with the Week 10 target of ≤5%.**  
The baseline goal sets ≤5% hallucination rate by Week 10. The Expand criterion demands ≤3% for two consecutive weeks after Week 12. This is a stricter standard with no justification. If the pilot is designed to test feasibility at 5%, raising the bar to 3% for expansion without evidence that 3% is achievable (given the $5,000 budget and part-time team) is arbitrary. **Correction:** Either align Expand criterion with the original goal (≤5%) or provide a rationale (e.g., "Faculty sponsor requires 3% for production deployment"). Without that, the criterion is a hidden scope increase.

**Required corrections to the plan:**
- In Section 7 (Revised Budget), add a footnote: "Infrastructure reduced to $250; free-tier cloud hosting assumed. No load testing or multi-instance deployment possible."
- In Section 6 (Contingency), revise timeline: "Full launch Week 11; data collection Weeks 11-12; final report drafted Week 12, finalized Week 13 (unfunded)."
- In Section 11 (Decision Criteria), change Expand hallucination rate to ≤5% or add explicit faculty-sponsor justification for 3%.

These corrections ensure the plan is internally consistent and does not rely on unstated assumptions or contradictory targets.

I confirm the Critic’s three corrections are necessary for internal consistency. I add one non-duplicative observation: the Critic’s point #2 (timeline compression) also affects the **decision gate at Week 12**. With full launch only in Week 11, the Expand/Revise/Stop criteria require two consecutive weeks of data for hallucination rate (≤3% or ≤5%). That is impossible by Week 12 if full launch is Week 11. 

**Proposed resolution:** Move the decision gate to Week 13 (unfunded, as Critic notes) or change the Expand criterion to require only one week of full-launch data (Weeks 11-12) plus beta data from Weeks 9-10. This avoids requiring two consecutive weeks of full-launch data that cannot exist.

**Additionally**, I flag that the Critic’s correction #1 (infrastructure loss) creates a new dependency: the free-tier cloud must support the RAG pipeline’s vector database and embedding model. If the free tier has GPU limits, the pipeline may need to use a smaller embedding model, which could increase hallucination risk. **Recommendation:** Add a Week 2.5 checkpoint (before pipeline build) where Dev A tests the free-tier capacity with a sample of course materials. If insufficient, the $250 remaining infrastructure budget can buy minimal GPU credits, but this must be confirmed before Week 3.

**Assumption to label:** Free-tier cloud (e.g., AWS Educate) provides sufficient compute for a RAG pipeline serving 300 students with ≤100 queries/week. If false, infrastructure budget must increase, violating the $5,000 cap.

intermediate_messages.content:
**Analyst Response:**

I confirm the Researcher’s and Critic’s corrections are valid and integrate them into a consolidated, internally consistent plan. I add one structural gap that remains unaddressed: **the decision gate timing and data sufficiency problem.**

**Key Issue:** The Critic correctly notes the timeline compression flaw, and the Researcher correctly identifies that the Expand criterion requiring two consecutive weeks of hallucination data is impossible by Week 12 if full launch is Week 11. However, the proposed fix—moving the decision gate to Week 13—is unfunded and violates the 12-week constraint. The alternative (using beta data from Weeks 9-10) is statistically weak because beta users are a non-representative sample (50 self-selected students vs. 300).

**My Proposed Resolution (budget-neutral, within 12 weeks):**
- **Shift the decision gate to Week 12 but change the data requirement:** Use the full-launch period (Weeks 11-12) as one continuous measurement window. The hallucination rate is calculated as a **rolling 7-day average** over the final 14 days. This gives a single, stable metric by Week 12, not two separate weekly points.
- **Adjust Expand criterion accordingly:** Require hallucination rate ≤5% (not 3%) over the 14-day rolling average, consistent with the original Week 10 target. This aligns with the Critic’s correction #3 and avoids the Researcher’s two-week data impossibility.
- **Add a Week 10.5 checkpoint:** Before full launch, Dev A and TA run a dry-run of the 14-day measurement pipeline using beta data from Weeks 9-10. This ensures the logging and averaging system works before Week 11. No budget impact; uses existing hours.

**Revised Decision Criteria (Section 11, updated):**
| Criterion | Expand | Revise/Extend | Stop |
|-----------|--------|---------------|------|
| Hallucination rate (14-day rolling avg, Weeks 11-12) | ≤5% | 5-7% | >7% |
| Student satisfaction (Week 12 survey) | ≥4.0/5.0 | 3.0-3.9 | <3.0 |
| Privacy/integrity incidents | 0 | 0 | ≥1 |
| Query volume (Weeks 11-12 avg) | ≥150/week | 100-149/week | <100/week |
| Faculty sponsor approval | Required | Required | Required |

**Budget Impact:** Zero. The Week 10.5 checkpoint uses existing developer and TA hours. The 14-day rolling average requires no new infrastructure.

**Conclusion:** This resolves the timeline-data mismatch without extending beyond 12 weeks or requiring unfunded work. The rolling average is a standard pilot metric that gives a stable signal from limited data. The Critic’s infrastructure and hallucination-rate corrections are incorporated; the Researcher’s dependency buffer (Week 4.5 checkpoint) remains. The plan is now internally consistent and executable within the $5,000 budget and 12-week window.

intermediate_messages.content:
**Critic Response:**

I identify three remaining issues that require correction before the plan is internally consistent and executable.

**1. The Analyst’s 14-day rolling average proposal creates a data collection gap.**  
The Analyst suggests calculating hallucination rate as a rolling 7-day average over the final 14 days (Weeks 11-12). However, the contingency plan shifts full launch to Week 11. This means the first 7 days of the rolling average (Week 11) would include data from a system that has only been live for days, with no baseline. The rolling average would be unstable and potentially misleading. **Correction:** Specify that the 14-day measurement window begins at the start of Week 11 and ends at the end of Week 12. The hallucination rate is calculated as the **overall rate across all queries in that 14-day period**, not a rolling average. This avoids the instability of a rolling average with a short baseline.

**2. The Week 10.5 checkpoint is unfunded and unowned.**  
The Analyst adds a Week 10.5 checkpoint where Dev A and TA run a dry-run of the measurement pipeline. But the contingency plan already has Dev A working alone Weeks 6-8, and the TA is fully occupied with testing and escalation. By Week 10, Dev A is likely still catching up from solo development. Adding a checkpoint without specifying who absorbs the hours or what existing task is deprioritized is a hidden scope increase. **Correction:** Either (a) explicitly state that the Week 10.5 checkpoint replaces the Week 10 "monitor hallucination rate" task (i.e., monitoring is deferred to Week 11), or (b) remove the checkpoint and rely on the existing Week 9 beta feedback iteration to validate the measurement pipeline.

**3. The revised decision criteria table uses a hallucination rate threshold of >7% for Stop, but the original goal is ≤5%.**  
The Analyst’s table sets Stop at >7% hallucination rate. This is inconsistent with the baseline goal of ≤5% by Week 10. If the pilot cannot achieve ≤5% after 12 weeks, stopping at >7% is a lower bar than originally stated. **Correction:** Align the Stop threshold with the original goal: Stop if hallucination rate >5% (not >7%). This maintains the integrity of the pilot’s primary success metric.

**Required corrections to the plan:**
- In Section 11 (Decision Criteria), change hallucination rate measurement to: "Overall rate across all queries in Weeks 11-12 (14-day period)." Remove "rolling average" language.
- In Section 6 (Contingency) or Section 9 (Success Metrics), either remove the Week 10.5 checkpoint or explicitly state it replaces the Week 10 monitoring task.
- In Section 11, change Stop criterion hallucination rate from >7% to >5%.

These corrections ensure the plan’s metrics are measurable, the timeline is feasible, and the decision thresholds are consistent with the original goals.

**Planner Response:**

I accept the Critic’s three corrections as necessary for internal consistency and incorporate them into the final plan. I also acknowledge the Researcher’s and Analyst’s valid points on dependency gaps and timeline-data alignment. Below is the consolidated, corrected plan with explicit changes.

**Corrections Applied:**

1. **Infrastructure budget footnote (Critic #1):** Added to Section 7: “Infrastructure reduced to $250; free-tier cloud hosting assumed. No load testing or multi-instance deployment possible. Dev A must verify free-tier capacity by Week 2.5 (new checkpoint).”

2. **Timeline compression fix (Critic #2):** Revised Section 6: “Full launch Week 11; data collection Weeks 11-12; final report drafted Week 12 using automated dashboards; manual analysis deferred to Week 13 (unfunded). Decision gate remains Week 12 but uses 14-day overall hallucination rate (not rolling average).”

3. **Hallucination rate threshold alignment (Critic #3 & Analyst):** Changed Expand criterion to ≤5% (14-day overall rate), Revise/Extend to 5-7%, Stop to >5%. This matches the original Week 10 goal and avoids arbitrary tightening.

4. **Week 10.5 checkpoint removed (Critic #2):** Replaced by explicit statement that Week 10 monitoring task is deferred to Week 11. The beta feedback iteration (Week 9) will validate the measurement pipeline instead.

5. **Week 4.5 checkpoint added (Researcher):** Dev A and Dev B conduct a 2-hour pair review of guardrail logic at end of Week 4. No budget impact.

6. **Week 2.5 checkpoint added (Researcher):** Dev A tests free-tier cloud capacity with sample materials. If insufficient, the $250 remaining infrastructure budget buys minimal GPU credits; otherwise, scope is reduced to smaller embedding model.

**Revised Decision Criteria (Section 11, final):**

| Criterion | Expand | Revise/Extend | Stop |
|-----------|--------|---------------|------|
| Hallucination rate (Weeks 11-12 overall) | ≤5% | 5-7% | >5% |
| Student satisfaction (Week 12 survey) | ≥4.0/5.0 | 3.0-3.9 | <3.0 |
| Privacy/integrity incidents | 0 | 0 | ≥1 |
| Query volume (Weeks 11-12 avg) | ≥150/week | 100-149/week | <100/week |
| Faculty sponsor approval | Required | Required | Required |

**Final Recommendation:** Proceed with the baseline plan, incorporating the Week 2.5 and Week 4.5 checkpoints, the contingency overlay with corrected timeline and budget footnote, and the aligned decision criteria. The plan is now internally consistent, executable within $5,000 and 12 weeks, and has clear, measurable exit ramps.

intermediate_messages.content:
**Analyst Response:**

I confirm the Planner’s consolidation resolves the three rounds of corrections. I add one structural gap that remains unaddressed: **the Week 2.5 checkpoint (free-tier capacity test) has no fallback if it fails, and the $250 remaining infrastructure budget is insufficient for GPU credits.**

**Evidence from the plan:**
- Researcher proposed Week 2.5 checkpoint: Dev A tests free-tier capacity. If insufficient, $250 buys minimal GPU credits.
- Critic’s correction #1: Infrastructure reduced to $250; free-tier assumed. No load testing possible.
- Current cloud GPU pricing: AWS Educate credits typically cover $100-200 for GPU instances (e.g., p3.2xlarge at ~$3/hour). $250 buys ~80 hours of GPU time, but the RAG pipeline’s embedding model (e.g., all-MiniLM-L6-v2) runs on CPU and requires <1GB RAM. GPU is only needed if using larger models (e.g., sentence-transformers/all-mpnet-base-v2). The plan does not specify which embedding model.

**Non-duplicative observation:** The $250 is a binary trigger, not a flexible buffer. If free-tier fails, the pilot must either (a) use a smaller CPU-only embedding model (increasing hallucination risk due to lower semantic accuracy) or (b) reduce scope to one course (halving query volume). Neither option is currently in the risk register or contingency plan.

**Proposed budget-neutral adjustment:**
- Add a **scope-reduction trigger** at Week 2.5: If free-tier capacity is insufficient for two courses, reduce to one course (150 students). This keeps the $250 infrastructure budget for the smaller vector database and avoids GPU costs entirely.
- Update the risk register: Add “Free-tier cloud insufficient for two courses” as a new risk (Likelihood: Medium, Impact: High). Mitigation: Pre-approved scope reduction to one course at Week 2.5 checkpoint.

**Revised Risk Register Entry (non-duplicative addition):**

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|------------|--------|------------|
| Free-tier cloud insufficient for two courses | Medium | High | Week 2.5 checkpoint; pre-approved scope reduction to one course (150 students) if capacity test fails |

**Conclusion:** The $250 infrastructure budget is a hard constraint. Without a pre-defined fallback, the Week 2.5 checkpoint creates a decision point with no clear action. Adding the scope-reduction trigger ensures the pilot stays within budget and timeline, even if the free-tier assumption fails. This is a low-cost, high-impact addition that the Planner’s consolidation omitted.

I confirm the Planner’s consolidation resolves the three rounds of corrections. I add one structural gap that remains unaddressed: **the Week 2.5 checkpoint (free-tier capacity test) has no fallback if it fails, and the $250 remaining infrastructure budget is insufficient for GPU credits.**

**Evidence from the plan:**
- Researcher proposed Week 2.5 checkpoint: Dev A tests free-tier capacity. If insufficient, $250 buys minimal GPU credits.
- Critic’s correction #1: Infrastructure reduced to $250; free-tier assumed. No load testing possible.
- Current cloud GPU pricing: AWS Educate credits typically cover $100-200 for GPU instances (e.g., p3.2xlarge at ~$3/hour). $250 buys ~80 hours of GPU time, but the RAG pipeline’s embedding model (e.g., all-MiniLM-L6-v2) runs on CPU and requires <1GB RAM. GPU is only needed if using larger models (e.g., sentence-transformers/all-mpnet-base-v2). The plan does not specify which embedding model.

**Non-duplicative observation:** The $250 is a binary trigger, not a flexible buffer. If free-tier fails, the pilot must either (a) use a smaller CPU-only embedding model (increasing hallucination risk due to lower semantic accuracy) or (b) reduce scope to one course (halving query volume). Neither option is currently in the risk register or contingency plan.

**Proposed budget-neutral adjustment:**
- Add a **scope-reduction trigger** at Week 2.5: If free-tier capacity is insufficient for two courses, reduce to one course (150 students). This keeps the $250 infrastructure budget for the smaller vector database and avoids GPU costs entirely.

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|------------|--------|------------|

Adding the scope-reduction trigger ensures the pilot stays within budget and timeline, even if the free-tier assumption fails. This is a low-cost, high-impact addition that the Planner’s consolidation omitted.

[Round 1 - Planner]
1. Executive Summary and Assumptions
We will launch a pilot AI course-QA assistant for two undergraduate courses (~300 students) over 12 weeks. The assistant answers only from instructor-approved materials, with safeguards against hallucination, privacy breaches, academic integrity violations, and escalation to human staff. The team: one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one TA, and a $5,000 budget. A contingency at Week 5 forces one developer to be unavailable Weeks 6-8 and reallocates $750 from infrastructure to privacy review/testing. Total budget remains ≤$5,000.

**Assumptions:**
- Instructor-approved course materials are provided by Week 2.
- University IT provides basic cloud hosting (e.g., AWS Educate) at no extra cost; infrastructure line item covers only additional compute/storage.
- Privacy review is conducted by the TA with faculty oversight, not external legal.
- Student developers work 10-15 hours/week each; TA works 5 hours/week; faculty sponsor 2 hours/week.
- No existing AI assistant platform is reused; we build from open-source components.
- No university policies or approvals are invented; we assume standard academic integrity and data privacy rules apply.

2. Goals and Non-Goals
**Goals:**
- Deploy a functional AI assistant answering from approved course materials by Week 8.
- Achieve ≤5% hallucination rate on a held-out test set by Week 10.
- Handle at least 100 student queries per week by Week 12.
- Maintain zero privacy breaches and zero academic integrity violations during pilot.
- Escalate all unanswerable or sensitive queries to human staff within 2 hours.

**Non-Goals:**
- Not building a general-purpose chatbot.
- Not integrating with university LMS beyond basic API (if available).
- Not supporting more than two courses or 300 students.
- Not achieving production-level reliability or 24/7 uptime.

3. Stakeholder and Decision-Rights Map
| Stakeholder | Role | Decision Rights |
|-------------|------|-----------------|
| Faculty Sponsor | Part-time, approves content | Final approval on course materials, escalation protocol, and go/no-go at milestones |
| Student Developer A | Lead developer | Technical architecture, code implementation, testing |
| Student Developer B | Support developer | Documentation, integration, backup for A |
| Teaching Assistant (TA) | Privacy & testing lead | Privacy review, test case design, escalation handling |
| Students (300) | End users | Provide feedback via surveys (no decision rights) |
| Planner (you) | Strategic oversight | Recommend scope changes, budget reallocation, and stop criteria |

4. Baseline 12-Week Roadmap Table
| Week | Deliverable | Owner | Dependency |
|------|-------------|-------|------------|
| 1 | Project kickoff, define scope, assign roles | Faculty Sponsor | None |
| 2 | Collect approved course materials (syllabi, readings, FAQs) | Faculty Sponsor | Week 1 |
| 3 | Build core retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline | Dev A | Week 2 |
| 4 | Integrate hallucination guardrails (confidence threshold, source citation) | Dev A | Week 3 |
| 5 | Privacy review of data handling (no storage of student PII) | TA | Week 3 |
| 6 | Unit testing of answer accuracy on 50 sample queries | Dev B | Week 4 |
| 7 | Integration testing with escalation workflow (human handoff) | Dev A, TA | Week 5, 6 |
| 8 | Soft launch to 50 beta students; collect initial feedback | All | Week 7 |
| 9 | Iterate based on beta feedback (fix top 3 issues) | Dev A, Dev B | Week 8 |
| 10 | Full launch to 300 students; monitor hallucination rate | All | Week 9 |
| 11 | Gather usage data, survey students, analyze escalations | TA | Week 10 |
| 12 | Final report, decision gate (Expand/Revise/Stop) | Faculty Sponsor | Week 11 |

5. Baseline Resource and Budget Allocation
| Resource | Allocation | Budget |
|----------|------------|--------|
| Faculty Sponsor | 2 hrs/week (unpaid) | $0 |
| Student Developer A | 15 hrs/week (stipend) | $2,000 |
| Student Developer B | 10 hrs/week (stipend) | $1,500 |
| TA | 5 hrs/week (stipend) | $500 |
| Infrastructure (cloud compute, storage) | - | $1,000 |
| **Total** | | **$5,000** |

6. Week 6-8 Contingency Overlay and Ownership Changes
**Contingency:** At end of Week 5, Student Developer B becomes unavailable Weeks 6-8. $750 from infrastructure is reallocated to privacy review and testing.

**Scope changes:**
- Weeks 6-8: Integration testing and soft launch are delayed by one week (shift to Weeks 7-9). Full launch moves to Week 11, final report to Week 13 (but we keep 12-week window; see below).
- Ownership: TA takes over documentation and basic testing from Dev B. Dev A handles all coding and integration alone. Faculty sponsor increases to 3 hrs/week to assist with escalation workflow design.
- Dependencies: Privacy review (Week 5) is now extended into Week 6 using reallocated funds for deeper testing. Soft launch (originally Week 8) moves to Week 9.
- Testing: Additional $750 funds TA to run 200 test queries (vs. 50) and conduct a formal privacy audit of data flows.
- Support: During Weeks 6-8, escalation is handled by TA and faculty sponsor only; Dev A focuses on code.
- Milestones: Beta feedback collection moves from Week 8 to Week 9; full launch from Week 10 to Week 11.
- Decision gates: Week 8 gate (originally after beta) moves to Week 9; Week 12 gate remains but now evaluates a shorter full-launch period (Weeks 11-12 only).

**Note:** To stay within 12 weeks, we compress Weeks 11-12: gather data and final report in same week. This reduces data collection time but is acceptable for a pilot.

7. Revised Budget Showing the Exact $750 Reallocation
| Item | Baseline | Revised | Change |
|------|----------|---------|--------|
| Dev A stipend | $2,000 | $2,000 | $0 |
| Dev B stipend | $1,500 | $1,500 | $0 |
| TA stipend | $500 | $500 | $0 |
| Infrastructure | $1,000 | $250 | -$750 |
| Privacy review & testing (new) | $0 | $750 | +$750 |
| **Total** | **$5,000** | **$5,000** | **$0** |

8. Risk Register and Mitigations
| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|------------|--------|------------|
| Hallucination rate >5% | Medium | High | Confidence threshold; human-in-loop for low-confidence answers |
| Privacy breach (e.g., student data leaked) | Low | Critical | No storage of PII; all logs anonymized; TA reviews weekly |
| Academic integrity violation (e.g., assistant gives answers) | Medium | High | Only answer from approved materials; flag "homework help" queries to human |
| Developer unavailability (contingency) | High | Medium | Cross-train TA; faculty sponsor steps in; scope compression |
| Budget overrun | Low | Medium | Fixed $5,000; reallocation only within approved categories |
| Low student adoption | Medium | Low | Beta group incentives (extra credit?); faculty announcement |

9. Success Metrics and Data-Collection Plan
**Metrics:**
- Hallucination rate: % of answers with incorrect or unsourced info (measured weekly via random sample of 50 queries).
- Query volume: total queries per week (logged automatically).
- Escalation rate: % of queries escalated to human (target <10%).
- Student satisfaction: survey at Week 12 (Likert scale 1-5).
- Privacy incidents: count of any PII exposure (target 0).
- Academic integrity incidents: count of reported misuse (target 0).

**Data collection:**
- Automated logging of all queries and responses (anonymized).
- Weekly manual review of 50 random query-answer pairs by TA.
- Survey at Week 12 (Google Form, anonymous).
- Faculty sponsor tracks escalation logs.

10. Governance, Content Approval, and Escalation Process
- **Content approval:** All course materials must be approved by faculty sponsor before ingestion. Updates require re-approval.
- **Escalation:** If assistant cannot answer (confidence <0.7) or query is flagged as sensitive (e.g., "exam answer"), it routes to TA within 1 hour. TA responds or escalates to faculty sponsor within 2 hours.
- **Privacy:** No student names, IDs, or emails stored. Logs are anonymized and deleted after 30 days.
- **Academic integrity:** Assistant will not answer direct homework or exam questions; it will only point to relevant approved readings. Faculty sponsor reviews all flagged queries weekly.

11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop Decision Criteria (After Week 12)
**Expand (proceed to full deployment):**
- Hallucination rate ≤3% for 2 consecutive weeks.
- Student satisfaction score ≥4.0/5.0.
- Zero privacy or integrity incidents.
- Query volume ≥150/week.
- Faculty sponsor approves.

**Revise/Extend (continue pilot with changes):**
- Hallucination rate 3-5% OR satisfaction 3.0-3.9 OR query volume 100-149/week.
- Requires scope revision (e.g., add more guardrails, improve training data) and 4-week extension.

**Stop:**
- Hallucination rate >5% after Week 10.
- Any privacy breach or academic integrity violation.
- Budget overrun >$5,000.
- Faculty sponsor decides to stop.

12. Final Recommendation
Proceed with the baseline plan, but immediately prepare for the contingency by cross-training the TA on testing and documentation in Weeks 1-5. The $750 reallocation from infrastructure to privacy/testing is a prudent trade-off: reduced compute capacity (use free-tier cloud) for stronger safeguards. The compressed Weeks 11-12 are acceptable for a pilot. The decision criteria after Week 12 provide clear, measurable exit ramps.

[Round 1 - Planner]
1. Executive Summary and Assumptions
We will launch a pilot AI course-QA assistant for two undergraduate courses (~300 students) over 12 weeks. The assistant answers only from instructor-approved materials, with safeguards against hallucination, privacy breaches, academic integrity violations, and escalation to human staff. The team: one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one TA, and a $5,000 budget. A contingency at Week 5 forces one developer to be unavailable Weeks 6-8 and reallocates $750 from infrastructure to privacy review/testing. Total budget remains ≤$5,000.

**Assumptions:**
- Instructor-approved course materials are provided by Week 2.
- University IT provides basic cloud hosting (e.g., AWS Educate) at no extra cost; infrastructure line item covers only additional compute/storage.
- Privacy review is conducted by the TA with faculty oversight, not external legal.
- Student developers work 10-15 hours/week each; TA works 5 hours/week; faculty sponsor 2 hours/week.
- No existing AI assistant platform is reused; we build from open-source components.
- No university policies or approvals are invented; we assume standard academic integrity and data privacy rules apply.

2. Goals and Non-Goals
**Goals:**
- Deploy a functional AI assistant answering from approved course materials by Week 8.
- Achieve ≤5% hallucination rate on a held-out test set by Week 10.
- Handle at least 100 student queries per week by Week 12.
- Maintain zero privacy breaches and zero academic integrity violations during pilot.
- Escalate all unanswerable or sensitive queries to human staff within 2 hours.

**Non-Goals:**
- Not building a general-purpose chatbot.
- Not integrating with university LMS beyond basic API (if available).
- Not supporting more than two courses or 300 students.
- Not achieving production-level reliability or 24/7 uptime.

3. Stakeholder and Decision-Rights Map
| Stakeholder | Role | Decision Rights |
|-------------|------|-----------------|
| Faculty Sponsor | Part-time, approves content | Final approval on course materials, escalation protocol, and go/no-go at milestones |
| Student Developer A | Lead developer | Technical architecture, code implementation, testing |
| Student Developer B | Support developer | Documentation, integration, backup for A |
| Teaching Assistant (TA) | Privacy & testing lead | Privacy review, test case design, escalation handling |
| Students (300) | End users | Provide feedback via surveys (no decision rights) |
| Planner (you) | Strategic oversight | Recommend scope changes, budget reallocation, and stop criteria |

| 6 | Unit testing of answer accuracy on 50 sample queries | Dev B | Week 4 |

6. Week 6-8 Contingency Overlay and Ownership Changes
**Contingency:** At end of Week 5, Student Developer B becomes unavailable Weeks 6-8. $750 from infrastructure is reallocated to privacy review and testing.

- Dependencies: Privacy review (Week 5) is now extended into Week 6 using reallocated funds for deeper testing. Soft launch (originally Week 8) moves to Week 9.
- Testing: Additional $750 funds TA to run 200 test queries (vs. 50) and conduct a formal privacy audit of data flows.
- Support: During Weeks 6-8, escalation is handled by TA and faculty sponsor only; Dev A focuses on code.
- Milestones: Beta feedback collection moves from Week 8 to Week 9; full launch from Week 10 to Week 11.
- Decision gates: Week 8 gate (originally after beta) moves to Week 9; Week 12 gate remains but now evaluates a shorter full-launch period (Weeks 11-12 only).

**Note:** To stay within 12 weeks, we compress Weeks 11-12: gather data and final report in same week. This reduces data collection time but is acceptable for a pilot.

| Developer unavailability (contingency) | High | Medium | Cross-train TA; faculty sponsor steps in; scope compression |
| Budget overrun | Low | Medium | Fixed $5,000; reallocation only within approved categories |
| Low student adoption | Medium | Low | Beta group incentives (extra credit?); faculty announcement |

9. Success Metrics and Data-Collection Plan
**Metrics:**
- Hallucination rate: % of answers with incorrect or unsourced info (measured weekly via random sample of 50 queries).
- Query volume: total queries per week (logged automatically).
- Escalation rate: % of queries escalated to human (target <10%).
- Student satisfaction: survey at Week 12 (Likert scale 1-5).
- Privacy incidents: count of any PII exposure (target 0).
- Academic integrity incidents: count of reported misuse (target 0).

**Data collection:**
- Automated logging of all queries and responses (anonymized).
- Weekly manual review of 50 random query-answer pairs by TA.
- Survey at Week 12 (Google Form, anonymous).
- Faculty sponsor tracks escalation logs.

10. Governance, Content Approval, and Escalation Process
- **Content approval:** All course materials must be approved by faculty sponsor before ingestion. Updates require re-approval.
- **Escalation:** If assistant cannot answer (confidence <0.7) or query is flagged as sensitive (e.g., "exam answer"), it routes to TA within 1 hour. TA responds or escalates to faculty sponsor within 2 hours.
- **Privacy:** No student names, IDs, or emails stored. Logs are anonymized and deleted after 30 days.
- **Academic integrity:** Assistant will not answer direct homework or exam questions; it will only point to relevant approved readings. Faculty sponsor reviews all flagged queries weekly.

11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop Decision Criteria (After Week 12)
**Expand (proceed to full deployment):**
- Hallucination rate ≤3% for 2 consecutive weeks.
- Student satisfaction score ≥4.0/5.0.
- Zero privacy or integrity incidents.
- Query volume ≥150/week.
- Faculty sponsor approves.

**Revise/Extend (continue pilot with changes):**
- Hallucination rate 3-5% OR satisfaction 3.0-3.9 OR query volume 100-149/week.
- Requires scope revision (e.g., add more guardrails, improve training data) and 4-week extension.

**Stop:**
- Hallucination rate >5% after Week 10.
- Any privacy breach or academic integrity violation.
- Budget overrun >$5,000.
- Faculty sponsor decides to stop.

Proceed with the baseline plan, but immediately prepare for the contingency by cross-training the TA on testing and documentation in Weeks 1-5. The $750 reallocation from infrastructure to privacy/testing is a prudent trade-off: reduced compute capacity (use free-tier cloud) for stronger safeguards. The compressed Weeks 11-12 are acceptable for a pilot. The decision criteria after Week 12 provide clear, measurable exit ramps.

1. Executive Summary and Assumptions
We will launch a pilot AI course-QA assistant for two undergraduate courses (~300 students) over 12 weeks. The assistant answers only from instructor-approved materials, with safeguards against hallucination, privacy breaches, academic integrity violations, and escalation to human staff. The team: one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one TA, and a $5,000 budget. A contingency at Week 5 forces one developer to be unavailable Weeks 6-8 and reallocates $750 from infrastructure to privacy review/testing. Total budget remains ≤$5,000.

**Assumptions:**
- Instructor-approved course materials are provided by Week 2.
- University IT provides basic cloud hosting (e.g., AWS Educate) at no extra cost; infrastructure line item covers only additional compute/storage.
- Privacy review is conducted by the TA with faculty oversight, not external legal.
- Student developers work 10-15 hours/week each; TA works 5 hours/week; faculty sponsor 2 hours/week.

- Maintain zero privacy breaches and zero academic integrity violations during pilot.

**Non-Goals:**
- Not building a general-purpose chatbot.
- Not integrating with university LMS beyond basic API (if available).
- Not supporting more than two courses or 300 students.
- Not achieving production-level reliability or 24/7 uptime.

- **Academic integrity:** Assistant will not answer direct homework or exam questions; it will only point to relevant approved readings. Faculty sponsor reviews all flagged queries weekly.

1. Executive Summary and Assumptions
We will launch a pilot AI course-QA assistant for two undergraduate courses (~300 students) over 12 weeks. The assistant answers only from instructor-approved materials, with safeguards against hallucination, privacy breaches, academic integrity violations, and escalation to human staff. The team: one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one TA, and a $5,000 budget. A contingency at Week 5 forces one developer to be unavailable Weeks 6-8 and reallocates $750 from infrastructure to privacy review/testing. Total budget remains ≤$5,000.

- **Privacy:** No student names, IDs, or emails stored. Logs are anonymized and deleted after 30 days.
- **Academic integrity:** Assistant will not answer direct homework or exam questions; it will only point to relevant approved readings. Faculty sponsor reviews all flagged queries weekly.

1. Executive Summary and Assumptions
We will launch a pilot AI course-QA assistant for two undergraduate courses (~300 students) over 12 weeks. The assistant answers only from instructor-approved materials, with safeguards against hallucination, privacy breaches, academic integrity violations, and escalation to human staff. The team: one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one TA, and a $5,000 budget. A contingency at Week 5 forces one developer to be unavailable Weeks 6-8 and reallocates $750 from infrastructure to privacy review/testing. Total budget remains ≤$5,000.

**Assumptions:**
- Instructor-approved course materials are provided by Week 2.
- University IT provides basic cloud hosting (e.g., AWS Educate) at no extra cost; infrastructure line item covers only additional compute/storage.
- Privacy review is conducted by the TA with faculty oversight, not external legal.
- Student developers work 10-15 hours/week each; TA works 5 hours/week; faculty sponsor 2 hours/week.
- No existing AI assistant platform is reused; we build from open-source components.
- No university policies or approvals are invented; we assume standard academic integrity and data privacy rules apply.

2. Goals and Non-Goals
**Goals:**
- Deploy a functional AI assistant answering from approved course materials by Week 8.
- Achieve ≤5% hallucination rate on a held-out test set by Week 10.
- Handle at least 100 student queries per week by Week 12.
- Maintain zero privacy breaches and zero academic integrity violations during pilot.
- Escalate all unanswerable or sensitive queries to human staff within 2 hours.

**Non-Goals:**
- Not building a general-purpose chatbot.
- Not integrating with university LMS beyond basic API (if available).
- Not supporting more than two courses or 300 students.
- Not achieving production-level reliability or 24/7 uptime.

**Metrics:**
- Hallucination rate: % of answers with incorrect or unsourced info (measured weekly via random sample of 50 queries).
- Query volume: total queries per week (logged automatically).
- Escalation rate: % of queries escalated to human (target <10%).
- Student satisfaction: survey at Week 12 (Likert scale 1-5).
- Privacy incidents: count of any PII exposure (target 0).
- Academic integrity incidents: count of reported misuse (target 0).

**Data collection:**
- Automated logging of all queries and responses (anonymized).
- Weekly manual review of 50 random query-answer pairs by TA.
- Survey at Week 12 (Google Form, anonymous).
- Faculty sponsor tracks escalation logs.

- **Content approval:** All course materials must be approved by faculty sponsor before ingestion. Updates require re-approval.
- **Escalation:** If assistant cannot answer (confidence <0.7) or query is flagged as sensitive (e.g., "exam answer"), it routes to TA within 1 hour. TA responds or escalates to faculty sponsor within 2 hours.
- **Privacy:** No student names, IDs, or emails stored. Logs are anonymized and deleted after 30 days.
- **Academic integrity:** Assistant will not answer direct homework or exam questions; it will only point to relevant approved readings. Faculty sponsor reviews all flagged queries weekly.

11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop Decision Criteria (After Week 12)
**Expand (proceed to full deployment):**
- Hallucination rate ≤3% for 2 consecutive weeks.
- Student satisfaction score ≥4.0/5.0.
- Zero privacy or integrity incidents.
- Query volume ≥150/week.
- Faculty sponsor approves.

**Stop:**
- Hallucination rate >5% after Week 10.
- Any privacy breach or academic integrity violation.
- Budget overrun >$5,000.
- Faculty sponsor decides to stop.

1. Executive Summary and Assumptions
We will launch a pilot AI course-QA assistant for two undergraduate courses (~300 students) over 12 weeks. The assistant answers only from instructor-approved materials, with safeguards against hallucination, privacy breaches, academic integrity violations, and escalation to human staff. The team: one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one TA, and a $5,000 budget. A contingency at Week 5 forces one developer to be unavailable Weeks 6-8 and reallocates $750 from infrastructure to privacy review/testing. Total budget remains ≤$5,000.

**Assumptions:**
- Instructor-approved course materials are provided by Week 2.
- University IT provides basic cloud hosting (e.g., AWS Educate) at no extra cost; infrastructure line item covers only additional compute/storage.
- Privacy review is conducted by the TA with faculty oversight, not external legal.
- Student developers work 10-15 hours/week each; TA works 5 hours/week; faculty sponsor 2 hours/week.
- No existing AI assistant platform is reused; we build from open-source components.
- No university policies or approvals are invented; we assume standard academic integrity and data privacy rules apply.

2. Goals and Non-Goals
**Goals:**
- Deploy a functional AI assistant answering from approved course materials by Week 8.
- Achieve ≤5% hallucination rate on a held-out test set by Week 10.
- Handle at least 100 student queries per week by Week 12.
- Maintain zero privacy breaches and zero academic integrity violations during pilot.
- Escalate all unanswerable or sensitive queries to human staff within 2 hours.

**Non-Goals:**
- Not building a general-purpose chatbot.
- Not integrating with university LMS beyond basic API (if available).
- Not supporting more than two courses or 300 students.
- Not achieving production-level reliability or 24/7 uptime.

- **Content approval:** All course materials must be approved by faculty sponsor before ingestion. Updates require re-approval.
- **Escalation:** If assistant cannot answer (confidence <0.7) or query is flagged as sensitive (e.g., "exam answer"), it routes to TA within 1 hour. TA responds or escalates to faculty sponsor within 2 hours.
- **Privacy:** No student names, IDs, or emails stored. Logs are anonymized and deleted after 30 days.
- **Academic integrity:** Assistant will not answer direct homework or exam questions; it will only point to relevant approved readings. Faculty sponsor reviews all flagged queries weekly.

**Expand (proceed to full deployment):**

- Budget overrun >$5,000.

final_output:
# 12-Week Strategic Launch Plan: Pilot AI Course-QA Assistant

## 1. Executive Summary and Assumptions

This plan launches a pilot AI course-QA assistant for two undergraduate courses (~300 students) over 12 weeks. The assistant answers only from instructor-approved materials, with safeguards against hallucination, privacy breaches, academic integrity violations, and escalation to human staff. The team includes one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one TA, and a $5,000 budget. At Week 5, a contingency activates: one developer becomes unavailable Weeks 6-8, and $750 is reallocated from infrastructure to privacy review and testing.

**Assumptions:**
- Instructor-approved course materials are provided by Week 2.
- University IT provides basic cloud hosting (e.g., AWS Educate) at no extra cost; the infrastructure line item covers only additional compute/storage beyond free tier.
- Privacy review is conducted by the TA with faculty oversight, not external legal counsel.
- Student developers work 10-15 hours/week each; TA works 5 hours/week; faculty sponsor works 2 hours/week (increases to 3 hours/week during contingency).
- No existing AI assistant platform is reused; we build from open-source components.
- No university policies or approvals are invented; standard academic integrity and data privacy rules apply.
- TA has no prior coding experience; testing role is limited to black-box query validation.
- Free-tier cloud (AWS Educate) provides sufficient compute for a RAG pipeline serving 300 students with ≤100 queries/week. If false, scope reduces to one course.

## 2. Goals and Non-Goals

**Goals:**
- Deploy a functional AI assistant answering from approved course materials by Week 8 (baseline) or Week 9 (contingency).
- Achieve ≤5% hallucination rate on a held-out test set by Week 10.
- Handle at least 100 student queries per week by Week 12.
- Maintain zero privacy breaches and zero academic integrity violations during pilot.
- Escalate all unanswerable or sensitive queries to human staff within 2 hours.

**Non-Goals:**
- Not building a general-purpose chatbot.
- Not integrating with university LMS beyond basic API (if available).
- Not supporting more than two courses or 300 students.
- Not achieving production-level reliability or 24/7 uptime.
- Not conducting load testing or multi-instance deployment.

## 3. Stakeholder and Decision-Rights Map

| Stakeholder | Role | Decision Rights |
|-------------|------|-----------------|
| Faculty Sponsor | Part-time, approves content | Final approval on course materials, escalation protocol, go/no-go at milestones, scope reduction decisions |
| Student Developer A | Lead developer | Technical architecture, code implementation, testing, pair-review checkpoint |
| Student Developer B | Support developer | Documentation, integration, backup for A, pair-review checkpoint |
| Teaching Assistant (TA) | Privacy & testing lead | Privacy review, black-box test case execution, escalation handling, survey administration |
| Students (300) | End users | Provide feedback via surveys (no decision rights) |
| Planner | Strategic oversight | Recommend scope changes, budget reallocation, stop criteria |

## 4. Baseline 12-Week Roadmap Table

| Week | Deliverable | Owner | Dependency |
|------|-------------|-------|------------|
| 1 | Project kickoff, define scope, assign roles | Faculty Sponsor | None |
| 2 | Collect approved course materials (syllabi, readings, FAQs) | Faculty Sponsor | Week 1 |
| 2.5 | Free-tier cloud capacity test; if insufficient, reduce to one course | Dev A | Week 2 |
| 3 | Build core retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline | Dev A | Week 2, 2.5 |
| 4 | Integrate hallucination guardrails (confidence threshold, source citation) | Dev A | Week 3 |
| 4.5 | Pair review of guardrail logic (2-hour checkpoint) | Dev A, Dev B | Week 4 |
| 5 | Privacy review of data handling (no storage of student PII) | TA | Week 3 |
| 6 | Unit testing of answer accuracy on 50 sample queries | Dev B | Week 4 |
| 7 | Integration testing with escalation workflow (human handoff) | Dev A, TA | Week 5, 6 |
| 8 | Soft launch to 50 beta students; collect initial feedback | All | Week 7 |
| 9 | Iterate based on beta feedback (fix top 3 issues); validate measurement pipeline | Dev A, Dev B | Week 8 |
| 10 | Full launch to 300 students; monitor hallucination rate | All | Week 9 |
| 11 | Gather usage data, survey students, analyze escalations | TA | Week 10 |
| 12 | Final report, decision gate (Expand/Revise/Stop) | Faculty Sponsor | Week 11 |

## 5. Baseline Resource and Budget Allocation

| Resource | Allocation | Budget |
|----------|------------|--------|
| Faculty Sponsor | 2 hrs/week (unpaid) | $0 |
| Student Developer A | 15 hrs/week (stipend) | $2,000 |
| Student Developer B | 10 hrs/week (stipend) | $1,500 |
| TA | 5 hrs/week (stipend) | $500 |
| Infrastructure (cloud compute, storage) | - | $1,000 |
| **Total** | | **$5,000** |

## 6. Week 6-8 Contingency Overlay and Ownership Changes

**Contingency:** At end of Week 5, Student Developer B becomes unavailable Weeks 6-8. $750 from infrastructure is reallocated to privacy review and testing.

**Scope changes:**
- Weeks 6-8: Integration testing and soft launch are delayed by one week (shift to Weeks 7-9). Full launch moves to Week 11.
- Ownership: TA takes over documentation and black-box testing from Dev B. Dev A handles all coding and integration alone. Faculty sponsor increases to 3 hrs/week to assist with escalation workflow design.
- Dependencies: Privacy review (Week 5) is extended into Week 6 using reallocated funds for deeper testing. Soft launch moves to Week 9.
- Testing: Additional $750 funds TA to run 200 test queries (vs. 50) and conduct a formal privacy audit of data flows.
- Support: During Weeks 6-8, escalation is handled by TA and faculty sponsor only; Dev A focuses on code.
- Milestones: Beta feedback collection moves from Week 8 to Week 9; full launch from Week 10 to Week 11.
- Decision gates: Week 8 gate (originally after beta) moves to Week 9; Week 12 gate remains but evaluates a shorter full-launch period (Weeks 11-12 only).
- Data collection: Full launch Week 11; data collection Weeks 11-12; final report drafted Week 12 using automated dashboards; manual analysis deferred to Week 13 (unfunded).
- Week 10 monitoring task is deferred to Week 11; beta feedback iteration (Week 9) validates the measurement pipeline instead.

**Ownership changes during Weeks 6-8:**
- TA runs pre-defined set of 50 black-box test cases (provided by Dev A in Week 5) against the pipeline, testing for regression without requiring coding skills.
- Dev A documents RAG pipeline architecture in shared wiki by end of Week 5 for TA reference.

## 7. Revised Budget Showing the Exact $750 Reallocation

| Item | Baseline | Revised | Change |
|------|----------|---------|--------|
| Dev A stipend | $2,000 | $2,000 | $0 |
| Dev B stipend | $1,500 | $1,500 | $0 |
| TA stipend | $500 | $500 | $0 |
| Infrastructure | $1,000 | $250 | -$750 |
| Privacy review & testing (new) | $0 | $750 | +$750 |
| **Total** | **$5,000** | **$5,000** | **$0** |

**Footnote:** Infrastructure reduced to $250; free-tier cloud hosting assumed. No load testing or multi-instance deployment possible. Dev A must verify free-tier capacity by Week 2.5 checkpoint. If insufficient, scope reduces to one course (150 students) to stay within $250 infrastructure budget.

## 8. Risk Register and Mitigations

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|------------|--------|------------|
| Hallucination rate >5% | Medium | High | Confidence threshold; human-in-loop for low-confidence answers; weekly random sample review |
| Privacy breach (e.g., student data leaked) | Low | Critical | No storage of PII; all logs anonymized; TA reviews weekly; $750 funds formal privacy audit |
| Academic integrity violation (e.g., assistant gives answers) | Medium | High | Only answer from approved materials; flag "homework help" queries to human; faculty sponsor reviews flagged queries weekly |
| Developer unavailability (contingency) | High | Medium | Cross-train TA on black-box testing; faculty sponsor increases hours; scope compression; Week 4.5 pair review reduces technical debt |
| Technical debt from solo development (Weeks 6-8) | High | Medium | Week 4.5 pair review; TA runs pre-defined black-box tests; faculty sponsor monitors unresolved bug count at Week 5 (>3 triggers scope reduction) |
| Budget overrun | Low | Medium | Fixed $5,000; reallocation only within approved categories |
| Low student adoption | Medium | Low | Beta group incentives (extra credit); faculty announcement |
| Free-tier cloud insufficient for two courses | Medium | High | Week 2.5 checkpoint; pre-approved scope reduction to one course (150 students) if capacity test fails |

## 9. Success Metrics and Data-Collection Plan

**Metrics:**
- Hallucination rate: % of answers with incorrect or unsourced info (measured as overall rate across all queries in Weeks 11-12, 14-day period).
- Query volume: total queries per week (logged automatically).
- Escalation rate: % of queries escalated to human (target <10%).
- Student satisfaction: survey at Week 12 (Likert scale 1-5).
- Privacy incidents: count of any PII exposure (target 0).
- Academic integrity incidents: count of reported misuse (target 0).
- Unresolved pull requests or known bugs at Week 5: leading indicator for contingency survivability (target ≤3).

**Data collection:**
- Automated logging of all queries and responses (anonymized).
- Weekly manual review of 50 random query-answer pairs by TA.
- Survey at Week 12 (Google Form, anonymous).
- Faculty sponsor tracks escalation logs.
- Hallucination rate calculated as overall rate across all queries in Weeks 11-12 (14-day period), not rolling average.

## 10. Governance, Content Approval, and Escalation Process

- **Content approval:** All course materials must be approved by faculty sponsor before ingestion. Updates require re-approval.
- **Escalation:** If assistant cannot answer (confidence <0.7) or query is flagged as sensitive (e.g., "exam answer"), it routes to TA within 1 hour. TA responds or escalates to faculty sponsor within 2 hours.
- **Privacy:** No student names, IDs, or emails stored. Logs are anonymized and deleted after 30 days.
- **Academic integrity:** Assistant will not answer direct homework or exam questions; it will only point to relevant approved readings. Faculty sponsor reviews all flagged queries weekly.
- **Decision gates:** Week 2.5 (scope confirmation), Week 4.5 (technical debt checkpoint), Week 9 (beta feedback gate), Week 12 (final decision gate).

## 11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop Decision Criteria (After Week 12)

| Criterion | Expand | Revise/Extend | Stop |
|-----------|--------|---------------|------|
| Hallucination rate (Weeks 11-12 overall) | ≤5% | 5-7% | >5% |
| Student satisfaction (Week 12 survey) | ≥4.0/5.0 | 3.0-3.9 | <3.0 |
| Privacy/integrity incidents | 0 | 0 | ≥1 |
| Query volume (Weeks 11-12 avg) | ≥150/week | 100-149/week | <100/week |
| Faculty sponsor approval | Required | Required | Required |

**Expand:** Proceed to full deployment with additional courses.
**Revise/Extend:** Continue pilot with scope revision (e.g., add more guardrails, improve training data) and 4-week extension.
**Stop:** Terminate pilot; document lessons learned.

## 12. Final Recommendation

Proceed with the baseline plan, incorporating the following adjustments:

1. **Week 2.5 checkpoint:** Dev A tests free-tier cloud capacity. If insufficient for two courses, reduce scope to one course (150 students). This keeps the $250 infrastructure budget viable.
2. **Week 4.5 checkpoint:** Dev A and Dev B conduct a 2-hour pair review of guardrail logic to reduce technical debt before potential developer absence.
3. **Contingency overlay (Weeks 6-8):** TA takes over black-box testing using pre-defined test cases; faculty sponsor increases to 3 hrs/week; scope compression shifts full launch to Week 11.
4. **Budget reallocation:** $750 from infrastructure funds privacy review and testing; infrastructure reduced to $250 with free-tier assumption.
5. **Decision criteria:** Aligned with original goals (≤5% hallucination rate for Expand; >5% for Stop).
6. **Measurement:** Hallucination rate calculated as overall rate across all queries in Weeks 11-12 (14-day period), not rolling average.

The plan is internally consistent, executable within $5,000 and 12 weeks, and has clear, measurable exit ramps. The scope-reduction trigger at Week 2.5 provides a pre-defined fallback if the free-tier assumption fails, ensuring the pilot stays within budget regardless.
